chunk_summary: This section explains how to interpret program coverage indicators in clinical contexts, emphasizing the limitations of reported data and the need for clinicians to verify actual intake and adherence to nutrition and fortification programs.
learning_objectives: Understand the limitations of program coverage indicators in clinical decision-making.
Learn to verify reported data, such as VAS doses, IFA adherence, and deworming compliance.
Identify discrepancies between distribution and actual intake in fortification programs.
Develop practical skills to translate coverage indicators into actionable clinical questions.
key_facts: Vitamin A Supplementation (VAS) is often reported as 'received in the last 6 months' rather than 'completed all nine doses.'
Iron and Folic Acid (IFA) coverage is frequently overestimated due to low adherence despite distribution.
National Deworming Day (NDD) indicators may double count doses if main-round and mop-up data are not reconciled.
Fortified rice monitoring tracks procurement or district supply but does not confirm individual consumption.
Iodized salt indicators differ between production-level iodine content and household-level adequacy due to storage practices.
reference_quote: ‘VAS is often reported as “received in the last 6 months” among a defined age band, not as “completed all nine doses.”’
document_key_topics: Interpreting program coverage indicators in clinical practice
Limitations of reported data in nutrition programs
Verification of adherence and intake in fortification programs
